headline: A Brush, Not a Blade: New Test Catches Oral Cancer in 60 Minutes
meta_description: Researchers at Queen Mary University of London have developed qMIDS, a painless brush test for oral cancer that matches surgical biopsy accuracy in under an ho…
summary_short: A simple mouth brush swab can now detect oral cancer with 95.5% accuracy in 60 minutes—without the pain of traditional biopsies that keep patients from returning for mon…
body:
Researchers at Queen Mary University of London, working with partners in India, have developed a painless alternative to surgical biopsy for detecting oral cancer. The test, called qMIDS (quantitative Malignancy Index Diagnostic System), uses a simple brush swab to analyze four genes linked to cancer, delivering results in under an hour. In a trial of 545 patients with potentially cancerous mouth lesions, it achieved 95.5 percent overall accuracy, with false-positive and false-negative rates each below five percent—matching the diagnostic power of a scalpel without any of the pain.

The real breakthrough isn't just accuracy; it's repeatability. Oral cancer cases have doubled worldwide since 1990, yet current monitoring relies on multiple surgical biopsies over months or years. The procedure is painful in an already sensitive area, causing many patients to skip follow-up appointments. A monitoring system patients avoid doesn't monitor anything. "This test gives clinicians a rapid, accurate, and non-invasive way to triage patients, and crucially, it can be repeated," said Muy-Teck Teh, an oral oncologist at Queen Mary University of London. "That means we can now monitor patients with persistent pre-malignant lesions regularly and systematically and pick up cancers much earlier."

The test works by analyzing mRNA expression from four specific genes in cells collected from a brush swab, with a control sample taken from healthy mouth tissue. The team was surprised by how well it performed. "We were genuinely astonished by the fact that the brush swab test performance is comparable to a microbiopsy," Teh said. The researchers expect the test could reach clinical use within two years.
quick_facts: Developed by Queen Mary University of London and Indian partners
95.5% accuracy in detecting oral cancer from brush swab
Results delivered in 60 minutes, no biopsy scalpel required
Trial included 545 patients with suspect mouth lesions
Expected in clinical use within two years
